sn22.90:1.1: On one occasion many elder monks were staying near Vārāṇasī in the Deer Park at Isipatana.
sn22.90:1.2: Then in the evening Ven. Channa left his seclusion and, taking his key, went from dwelling to dwelling, saying to the elder monks,
sn22.90:1.3: “May the venerable elders exhort me, may the venerable elders teach me, may the venerable elders give me a Dhamma talk so that I might see the Dhamma.”
sn22.90:2.1: When this was said, the elder monks said to Ven. Channa,
sn22.90:3.1: Then the thought occurred to Ven. Channa,
sn22.90:3.3: form is inconstant, feeling is inconstant,
sn22.90:3.7: form is not-self, feeling is not-self,
sn22.90:3.11: all fabrications are inconstant; all phenomena are not-self.
sn22.90:3.12: But still my mind does not leap up, grow confident, steadfast, & released [alternate reading: firm] in the pacification of all fabrications, the relinquishing of all acquisitions, the ending of craving, dispassion, cessation, unbinding.
sn22.90:3.14: and my intellect pulls back, thinking,
sn22.90:3.16: But this thought doesn’t occur to one who sees the Dhamma.
sn22.90:3.17: So who might teach me the Dhamma so that I might see the Dhamma?”
sn22.90:4.1: Then the thought occurred to Ven. Channa,
sn22.90:4.2: “This Ven. Ānanda is staying near Kosambī at Ghosita’s monastery. He has been praised by the Teacher and is esteemed by his observant companions in the holy life. He is capable of teaching me the Dhamma
sn22.90:4.3: so that I might see the Dhamma, and I have sudden trust in him.
sn22.90:4.4: Why don’t I go to Ven. Ānanda?”
sn22.90:4.5: So, setting his lodgings in order and taking his robes & bowl, Ven. Channa went to Kosambī to where Ven. Ānanda was staying at Ghosita’s monastery. On arrival, he exchanged courteous greetings with Ven. Ānanda. After an exchange of friendly greetings & courtesies, he sat to one side. As he was sitting there, he
sn22.90:5.1: (told Ven. Ānanda what had happened and added),
sn22.90:7.7: “May Ven. Ānanda exhort me, may Ven. Ānanda teach me, may Ven. Ānanda give me a Dhamma talk so that I might see the Dhamma.”
sn22.90:8.1: “Even this much makes me feel gratified & satisfied with Ven. Channa, that he opens up & breaks down his stubbornness.
sn22.90:8.3: You are capable of understanding the Dhamma.”
sn22.90:8.4: Then a sudden great rapture & joy welled up in Ven. Channa at the thought, “So I am capable of understanding the Dhamma!”
sn22.90:8.5: “Face-to-face with the Blessed One have I heard this, friend Channa.
sn22.90:9.1: Face-to-face with him have I learned the exhortation he gave to the monk Kaccāna Gotta [SN 12:15]:
sn22.90:9.2: ‘By & large, Kaccāna, this world is supported by [takes as its object] a polarity, that of existence & non-existence.
sn22.90:9.3: But when one sees the origination of the world as it has come to be with right discernment, “non-existence” with reference to the world does not occur to one.
sn22.90:9.4: When one sees the cessation of the world as it has come to be with right discernment, “existence” with reference to the world does not occur to one.
sn22.90:9.5: “‘By & large, Kaccāna, this world is in bondage to attachments, clingings (sustenances), & biases.
sn22.90:9.6: But one such as this does not get involved with or cling to these attachments, clingings, fixations of awareness, biases, or obsessions; nor is he resolved on “my self.”
sn22.90:9.7: He has no uncertainty or doubt that, when there is arising, only stress is arising; and that when there is passing away, only stress is passing away.
sn22.90:9.8: In this, one’s knowledge is independent of others.
sn22.90:9.9: It is to this extent, Kaccāna, that there is right view.
sn22.90:9.11: “Everything doesn’t exist”: That is a second extreme.
sn22.90:9.12: Avoiding these two extremes, the Tathāgata teaches the Dhamma via the middle:
sn22.90:9.13: From ignorance as a requisite condition come fabrications.
sn22.90:9.14: From fabrications as a requisite condition comes consciousness. From consciousness as a requisite condition comes name-&-form. From name-&-form as a requisite condition come the six sense media. From the six sense media as a requisite condition comes contact. From contact as a requisite condition comes feeling. From feeling as a requisite condition comes craving. From craving as a requisite condition comes clinging/sustenance. From clinging/sustenance as a requisite condition comes becoming. From becoming as a requisite condition comes birth. From birth as a requisite condition, then aging & death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, distress, & despair come into play.
sn22.90:9.15: Such is the origination of this entire mass of stress & suffering.
sn22.90:9.16: “‘Now from the remainderless fading & cessation of that very ignorance comes the cessation of fabrications. From the cessation of fabrications comes the cessation of consciousness. From the cessation of consciousness comes the cessation of name-&-form. From the cessation of name-&-form comes the cessation of the six sense media. From the cessation of the six sense media comes the cessation of contact. From the cessation of contact comes the cessation of feeling. From the cessation of feeling comes the cessation of craving. From the cessation of craving comes the cessation of clinging/ sustenance. From the cessation of clinging/sustenance comes the cessation of becoming. From the cessation of becoming comes the cessation of birth. From the cessation of birth, then aging & death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, distress, & despair all cease.
sn22.90:9.17: Such is the cessation of this entire mass of stress & suffering.’’’
sn22.90:10.1: “That’s how it is, friend Ānanda, for those who have companions in the holy life like Ven. Ānanda—sympathetic, helpful, exhorting, & teaching.
sn22.90:10.2: Just now, for me, listening to Ven. Ānanda’s Dhamma-teaching, has the Dhamma been penetrated.”
